notes.text: Amalgams that appear elevated, flat, and shiny years later usually mean surrounding enamel/dentin was lost to acid erosion (GERD, frequent acidic diet/drinks), so restorations stand proud. notes.text: For deep caries possibly needing crown lengthening: excavate first to judge restorability and pulp status; obtain bitewings to assess remaining tooth structure, biologic width, and ferrule needs; complete RCT if indicated; then perform surgical crown lengthening before definitive buildup/crown. Doing RCT before radiographic assessment of remaining structure, or lengthening before knowing restorability, can lead to non-restorable roots or unnecessary surgery.

notes.text: In a 5-year-old with negative behavior and multiple occlusal lesions, professionally applied fluoride as a varnish wiped/painted on is preferred: short chair time, no trays, low gag risk, and lower systemic ingestion risk than gel. Tray-delivered fluoride gel needs longer retention and cooperation and raises ingestion risk in uncooperative kids. “Wipe with gel” still uses a less sticky, higher-ingestion-risk vehicle than varnish.

notes.text: In classic occlusion teaching, “organic occlusion” means mutually protected occlusion: posteriors take load in MIP and protect anteriors, while anterior guidance discludes posteriors in excursion. Centric and eccentric describe jaw positions/movements, not the protective scheme. Group function is an alternative lateral scheme (shared posterior contact in working excursion), not the synonym for organic/mutually protected occlusion.

notes.text: A positive Paul–Bunnell (heterophile antibody) test supports infectious mononucleosis from EBV; it is not used to diagnose HSV, HPV, or SLE.

notes.text: An isolated enamel mass in a furcation is an enamel pearl (ectopic enamel); it differs from dens invaginatus and from generalized enamel hypoplasia patterns.

notes.text: Oral hygiene should begin with eruption of the first primary teeth (usually primary incisors); waiting for molars or permanent first molars delays plaque control and caries prevention.

notes.text: Large restorations complicate orthodontics mainly because bond strength to restorative surfaces is less predictable and restorations or brackets often fracture or fail during debonding and appliance removal. Orthodontic forces do not systematically “speed” or “slow” biologic tooth movement solely because a filling is large. GI/diet investigation is unrelated to appliance mechanics on heavily restored teeth.

notes.text: Addition silicone (PVS) is the preferred final crown impression material: high accuracy, excellent dimensional stability, and fine surface detail for dies and margins. Alginate lacks the precision and stability for definitive fixed prosthodontics. Agar can capture detail but is technique-sensitive and less practical for routine crown work. GI/diet investigation is not an impression material.

notes.text: When negative reinforcement and parental behavior methods fail for persistent thumb-sucking, a fixed habit-breaking appliance (palatal crib) physically interrupts digit placement and is the standard dental next step. notes.text: Acid etching demineralizes enamel and opens a microporous surface so resin tags form micromechanical retention and a continuous enamel–composite seal. That seal is what reduces microleakage at margins. Temperature of the tooth is irrelevant to etch; shade matching is driven by opacity/layering, not etch alone; dentin stain is not primarily reduced by etch without proper bonding and isolation.

notes.text: Caries risk is assessed before periodontal therapy because scaling and surgery often expose root surfaces; high-risk patients then develop root caries and marked dentin hypersensitivity on newly uncovered cervical root. Loss of coronal structure and pocket-depth reduction are treatment outcomes or separate restorative issues, not the main reason to stage caries-risk planning first. GI/diet alone does not replace caries-risk assessment before perio.

notes.text: A fractured cusp on a vital tooth needs cuspal coverage to protect remaining structure; a ceramic onlay restores the missing cusp while conserving more tooth than a full-coverage crown. Direct composite alone often underprotects a missing cusp under load. Full ceramic crown is more aggressive when an onlay can provide coverage. GI/diet investigation does not restore the fractured cusp.

notes.text: Among the listed choices, only Resilon is an obturation-related root-filling system; the other options (etched porcelain, occlusal contact scheme, GI workup) are not obturation materials. Clinically, true resin allergy argues against resin-based root fillers/sealers and favors gutta-percha with a non-resin sealer when available—so Resilon is the only on-list obturation answer, not the ideal biocompatible choice.

notes.text: Subgingival overhanging margins create plaque traps in a low-oxygen niche that favors anaerobic gram-negative periodontal pathogens and inflammation. Smooth subgingival margins are less retentive; supragingival overhangs still trap plaque but sit above the sulcus and are less associated with subgingival gram-negative blooms. GI/diet investigation does not explain the microbial association with restorative contour errors.

notes.text: Wits appraisal measures the linear distance between projections of points A and B onto the functional occlusal plane (not the anatomic occlusal plane). It assesses sagittal jaw discrepancy independent of cranial-base angle variation.

notes.text: Vertical root fracture is confirmed definitively by direct visualization (surgical exploration/flap or extraction inspection). CBCT and multiple PAs can support suspicion but do not always prove VRF; exploratory surgery is the gold-standard confirmation.

notes.text: Large open interproximal contacts are cleaned most effectively with an interdental brush sized to the embrasure. Standard floss and superfloss work better in tight contacts; a conventional toothbrush alone does not clean mid-embrasure plaque well.

notes.text: Recession-related dentin hypersensitivity localizes to exposed cervical root dentin near the CEJ, where open tubules face thermal/tactile stimuli. Mid-root bulk is usually covered by gingiva or cementum and is not the classic sensitive site. Cervical crown enamel lacks patent dentinal tubules. GI/diet may contribute to erosion but the anatomic site of recession hypersensitivity is cervical root.

notes.text: Carisolv is a chemomechanical caries-removal system (NaOCl-based gel with amino acids) that softens infected dentin for selective hand excavation without rotary cutting as the primary method. Etched porcelain and occlusal contact schemes are restorative/occlusal concepts, not chemo-mechanical excavation agents. GI/diet investigation does not remove carious dentin.

notes.text: Significant renal impairment is a classic caution for fluoride exposure because reduced renal clearance raises systemic fluoride load. Among listed organ systems, renal disease is the keyed reason to avoid/limit fluoride varnish or systemic fluoride decisions per protocol.

notes.text: Silver points corrode in tissue fluids; corrosion products are toxic and associated with inflammation and treatment failure. Corrosion-related toxicity is the classic disadvantage that led to abandonment of silver points.

notes.text: Transillumination (fiber-optic light) helps visualize crack lines as they block light transmission. Bite-on-release pain supports cracked-tooth syndrome clinically, but among options, transillumination is the direct confirmatory chairside optical test for a crack.

notes.text: Early removable partial denture failures commonly involve clasp problems (deformation, fracture, loss of retention, or tissue trauma from ill-fitting clasps). Connectors and bases fail too, but clasps are the most frequent early mechanical/clinical complaint.

notes.text: Microfilled composites polish well but have lower strength/fracture resistance for load-bearing or large veneer redo cases. Modern nanofilled/nanohybrid composites combine polishability with improved mechanical properties and are preferred for redoing direct composite veneers.

notes.text: Recession limited to buccal surfaces of a quadrant without interproximal attachment loss is a mucogingival deformity/condition of buccal recession (Cairo RT1-type pattern), not interproximal recession deformity and not automatically periodontitis stage II/III.

notes.text: Tranexamic acid is an antifibrinolytic used to reduce bleeding in hemophilia and other bleeding disorders (systemic or topical oral rinse). It is not used to treat stroke, DVT, or valve disease—those settings may even caution antifibrinolytics.

notes.text: A severely autistic adult who lacks decision-making capacity cannot give valid informed consent; the legally authorized surrogate (typically the father/guardian who accompanies and has authority) consents. Brother or doctor do not automatically hold consent power.

notes.text: Cobalt-chromium has a much higher modulus of elasticity (stiffer) than gold alloys, so thin major connectors and bases resist flexure and fracture better under functional load. High stiffness, not porcelain etching or occlusion scheme, explains superior fracture resistance of Co-Cr frameworks.

notes.text: Cusp-tip reduction is indicated when the same cusp tip participates in both centric (CR/ICP) and eccentric interferences—selective grinding hierarchy: if a contact is heavy in both, reduce the cusp tip rather than only fossae or only incline planes.

notes.text: At denture insertion, first verify fit/adaptation and pressure areas (base seating), then borders, then occlusion. Checking occlusion before confirming the denture seats correctly misattributes errors. Fit is the first check among the listed sequence.

notes.text: Orthopnea requiring multiple pillows plus dependent leg swelling is classic congestive heart failure (fluid overload and elevated filling pressures). Diabetes alone does not produce this orthopnea-edema cluster.

notes.text: A beefy red atrophic tongue often reflects nutritional deficiency (B vitamins, iron, etc.). Management centers on improving nutrition and correcting the deficiency after appropriate workup—not antiseptics alone as the primary answer.

notes.text: Gustatory sweating and pain while eating after parotid surgery is Frey syndrome (auriculotemporal nerve misdirection: parasympathetic fibers reinnervate sweat glands). Sinusitis, syphilis, and osteomyelitis do not produce meal-triggered facial sweating.

notes.text: Target/“bull’s-eye” or concentric-ring skin lesions are hallmark of erythema multiforme (EM). Sebaceous glands and mucoepidermoid carcinoma do not produce that cutaneous target morphology.

notes.text: Powder dentifrices are typically more abrasive than gels/pastes and are associated with greater toothbrush abrasion of dentin/cementum when used aggressively. Gel dentifrices are generally less abrasive.

notes.text: Celluloid/strip crowns are used in pediatric dentistry to restore primary anterior teeth after caries or trauma, including incisal fractures, when a full-coverage esthetic form is needed with composite. They are not porcelain veneers or diet counseling.

notes.text: Acetaminophen is contraindicated by allergy. Post-MI patients need careful NSAID selection; among common NSAIDs naproxen has historically been considered relatively preferable for cardiovascular risk versus diclofenac/high-dose ibuprofen when an NSAID is unavoidable—matching the keyed choice.

notes.text: Pilocarpine is a cholinergic sialogogue indicated for xerostomia (e.g., Sjögren syndrome or post-radiation dry mouth) when residual salivary function exists. The clinical indication among options is xerostomia, not the unrelated distractors.

notes.text: Wheezing and dyspnea during a lengthy rubber-dam procedure most often reflect an acute asthma exacerbation (stress, posture, possible irritant triggers). True latex anaphylaxis is also possible but the keyed answer among options is asthma; manage airway and bronchodilators.

notes.text: For an avulsed permanent incisor with ~45 minutes extraoral time, replant and use a flexible (non-rigid) splint. Closed-apex teeth usually develop pulp necrosis—start root canal treatment within about 7–10 days (pulpectomy pathway), not rigid long-term splinting alone.

notes.text: Expansion of the mandible/jaw with an ‘orange peel’ or ground-glass radiographic pattern is classic for fibrous dysplasia. Periapical/florid cemento-osseous dysplasia are typically non-expansile jaw lesions; ossifying fibroma is a defined neoplasm with different borders.

notes.text: For a young child, open bite and unilateral crossbite from thumb-sucking often improve if the habit stops early; many protocols delay interceptive habit appliances until later mixed dentition (~7–8+) if the habit is self-limiting. The keyed plan is observation/return around age 8 rather than immediate pacifier or crib in this stem.

notes.text: A midline diastema appearing during rapid maxillary expansion is expected as the midpalatal suture opens; continue planned activations. It is not an emergency to reverse the screw or remove the appliance solely for that physiologic space.

notes.text: Quad helix expands the maxilla to correct posterior crossbite and can be modified with a crib/habit elements; it addresses transverse discrepancy better than a pure palatal crib (habit only). Haas is a jackscrew expander; W-arch is similar but quad helix is the common keyed choice for this combo.

notes.text: Gingival enlargement classified by extent: papillary, marginal, or diffuse. Inflammation that extends to the vestibular fold involves attached gingiva broadly and is termed diffuse gingival enlargement, not limited marginal or papillary enlargement.

notes.text: Internal (inflammatory) root resorption appears as a radiolucent expansion of the pulp chamber/canal outline. It is not peripheral radiopacity around the root; the keyed radiographic feature is radiolucency continuous with the pulp cavity.

notes.text: Emergency dental care after recent MI may proceed with medical precautions (BP monitoring as requested). Clopidogrel (Plavix) is an antiplatelet—do not order INR (that is for warfarin). Routine antibiotic prophylaxis is not indicated solely for MI/Plavix; do not automatically defer true emergency care.

notes.text: Insufficient occlusal reduction for a PFM leaves the restoration overcontoured or the porcelain/metal too thin, producing occlusal dysfunction, high contacts, or porcelain fracture risk. The primary clinical consequence highlighted is occlusal dysfunction from inadequate reduction space.

notes.text: Class V (cervical facial/lingual) restorations generally do not require a matrix; contour and isolation are managed with hand instruments, cervical matrices only if needed, but tofflemire/sectional matrices are for proximal boxes. ‘No need’ for a routine matrix is correct.

notes.text: Axial CBCT sections show how many bony walls remain around an infrabony defect. A three-wall defect (three remaining walls) is the classic morphology assessed on axial imaging and has the best regenerative prognosis.

notes.text: Koilonychia (spoon nails), smooth red tongue, pallor, and microcytic anemia (low RBC and low MCV) point to iron-deficiency anemia. Pernicious anemia is macrocytic (high MCV) from B12 deficiency, so it is excluded.

notes.text: Severe xerostomia from minor salivary disease makes mucosa-borne RPDs poorly retained and traumatic. Tooth- or implant-supported fixed options avoid reliance on saliva; implant-supported partial FPD is preferred for replacing two premolars when abutments/implants allow.

notes.text: Before a definitive impression for a new RPD, tissues distorted by an old prosthesis need rest. Leaving the old RPD out about 24 hours (tissue rest) is the common exam interval among the listed choices so mucosa can rebound before impression.

notes.text: Maxillary major connectors should clear free gingival margins by about 6 mm to avoid plaque trapping and gingival inflammation. About 3 mm is the usual mandibular clearance figure, not maxillary.

notes.text: Myxedema coma is severe hypothyroidism with altered mental status; hypothermia is the hallmark early/clinical sign from failed thermoregulation. Bradycardia occurs but hypothermia is classically cited as the key first/cardinal finding.

notes.text: After scaling and root planing, inflamed connective tissue and long junctional epithelium remodel: pocket depth and inflammatory infiltrate first regress, then collagen organization and attachment quality increase during healing.

notes.text: Cavit is a temporary zinc oxide–based endodontic provisional only. It lacks strength, long-term seal, and wear resistance for definitive restoration and must be replaced with a proper temporary or permanent material after treatment. It is not a final restorative option; the other listed distractors (GI workup, etched porcelain, occlusal contact scheme) are not provisional materials at all.

notes.text: Primary upper anterior that darkens after trauma then spontaneously lightens suggests transient pulp hemorrhage/resorption of pigment with recovering vitality. Observation/follow-up with vitality testing is preferred over immediate invasive treatment.

notes.text: When IE prophylaxis is indicated and the patient cannot take oral meds, parenteral ampicillin (IM/IV, typically 2 g) is the standard substitute. Oral amoxicillin, azithromycin, or clindamycin apply only if oral dosing is possible.

notes.text: Rigidly connecting a natural tooth to an implant loads the tooth’s PDL differently from the osseointegrated implant. Relative intrusion or mobility of the tooth tends to break the cement seal on the tooth abutment rather than equally stressing both units.

notes.text: Multiple caries and abscesses in a young child define severe early childhood caries as the clinical dental diagnosis. Neglect may be a social risk factor, but the question asks for the disease diagnosis, not the type of maltreatment.

notes.text: Toughness is the energy absorbed per unit volume up to fracture (area under the stress–strain curve). Modulus of elasticity is stiffness (slope of the elastic portion), not total energy to break.

notes.text: Plaque index ~40% with BOP ~20% means substantial plaque remains despite some cleaning effort and moderate inflammation. That pattern matches inadequate technique with attempted brushing rather than total non-brushing or perfect technique without brushing.

notes.text: Chronic mouth breathing often links to maxillary constriction and crossbite/narrow arch. Orthopedic maxillary expansion improves nasal airway space and transverse arch form as the main skeletal/orthodontic treatment hinge.

notes.text: Among the garbled options, the item targets HBV transmission context. HBV is blood- and body-fluid–borne (percutaneous/mucosal), not a classic respiratory droplet pathogen like influenza; the keyed option is the only HBV-related choice in this bank item.

notes.text: Carbohydrate-driven demineralization under or around a prosthesis produces white-spot/caries lesions often described with denture hygiene problems. Caries is the pathologic process; diet investigation is adjunctive history, not the lesion type itself.

notes.text: Porcelain is brittle under tension but relatively strong under compression. PFM design (metal substructure support, coping geometry) keeps porcelain under compressive loading in function to reduce tensile cracking of the veneer. Tensile and shear stresses are the modes that more readily initiate ceramic fracture; GI/diet has no role in porcelain–metal force design.

notes.text: After traumatic loss of an upper central (#21), a bone-level implant allows platform placement for soft-tissue thickness and esthetic emergence in the anterior maxilla. Tissue-level designs are less flexible for high-smile esthetic zones.

notes.text: Generalized brown intrinsic discoloration that fluoresces yellow under ultraviolet light is classic for tetracycline staining during tooth formation. Extrinsic stains and most other intrinsic defects lack this UV fluorescence pattern.

notes.text: Intact restoration with tight contacts, normal probing depth, food impaction, and gingival pain suggests closed embrasures (decreased embrasure space) that trap food, not periodontal pocketing or plaque-induced disease alone.

notes.text: Perineural invasion on histology of a salivary neoplasm is a signature of adenoid cystic carcinoma, explaining pain and local spread along nerves. Mucoepidermoid carcinoma more often shows cystic/mucous and epidermoid cells without that classic perineural predilection.

notes.text: Generalized hypocalcified enamel needs masking of color and reinforcement of structure; composite veneers restore esthetics and protect weak enamel. Microabrasion/bleaching alone do not adequately treat substantial hypocalcification.

notes.text: Legal and clinical quality of dental records hinges first on completeness: history, findings, diagnosis, consent, treatment, and follow-up must all be present. Objectivity and specificity matter but incomplete notes fail documentation standards even if tidy.

notes.text: A nonvital or endodontically symptomatic tooth with an apical abscess and radiographic apical pathosis is primary endodontic disease. Primary periodontal disease would show deep pockets and crestal bone loss as the driver without a primary pulp source.

notes.text: Cast bridge distortion occurs when seating or occlusal forces push the metal beyond its elastic limit into plastic deformation, so the framework no longer returns to the intended fit. Pure occlusal discrepancy without plastic deformation does not permanently warp the casting.

notes.text: Amalgam marginal ditching deeper than about 0.5 mm indicates breakdown and leakage risk with possible recurrent caries under the margin. Patching the ditch with composite over old amalgam is unreliable (interface failure, continued leakage). Full replacement of the defective amalgam is the appropriate management when ditch depth exceeds that threshold and the restoration is clinically unsatisfactory.

notes.text: Divergent multi-unit implants need an open-tray (pick-up) transfer with two-piece copings so the impression captures true implant angulation without locking a closed-tray coping path. Closed-tray transfers fail when paths of draw diverge.

notes.text: In lateral excursion, posterior group-function contacts (especially heavy balancing/working posterior loading) are more destructive than mutually protected canine guidance with disclusion of posteriors. Shallow inclines and canine guidance reduce lateral posterior stress.

notes.text: For amalgam cores/build-ups, self-threading pins provide the greatest retention in dentin among classic pin types. Friction-lock and cemented pins retain less; self-shear describes a pin design feature, not the preferred retention class for endodontically treated molars needing pins.

notes.text: Decreased freeway space means occlusal vertical dimension is too high (teeth meet too soon). Selective grinding of denture teeth (lab or chairside) can restore freeway space; relieving the intaglio alone does not correct OVD, and remake is not always first step.

notes.text: Whole saliva contains electrolytes (including potassium), secretory IgA (antibodies), and antimicrobial proteins such as lysozyme. notes.text: A broken shallow amalgam in a small cavity is often best replaced with bonded composite for conservative tooth-colored repair with adhesive retention. Full amalgam replacement is unnecessary if remaining structure allows a bonded approach; GIC is weaker for stress-bearing areas.

notes.text: In a high-caries pediatric patient sealing permanent molars, filled resin/flowable composite sealants offer better wear resistance than unfilled resin while still penetrating fissures when isolation is adequate. GIC is an alternative under moisture risk but the keyed choice is filled resin sealant.

notes.text: Adolescent deep bite can be reduced with a removable appliance incorporating an anterior bite plane that allows posterior eruption and intrusion/retroclination of incisors. notes.text: Crown lengthening when keratinized gingiva is inadequate needs an apically repositioned flap (often with osseous recontouring) to expose tooth structure while preserving or relocating a band of attached gingiva. Simple gingivectomy removes keratinized tissue and is contraindicated.
